author: @andrewharpin6749
text:
Hi Rob, I think there's a balance to be drawn,  I see your perspective regarding "not providing possible solutions" and depending on the problem and the complexity of the system, you are correct for a simple system that is most likely correct.

For a more complex system, this may not be so straight forward, and what someone may be highlighting is a symptom/issue, rather than the root cause problem.

Now you may argue "why have you such a complex system?" and you're right, but sometimes those complex systems are required.

Now traditionally (especially when dealing with external parties), it's getting the acceptance of "there is a problem". I think the benefit comes when those parties work together to solve the issue, whatever that may be.

I think it's important for the individual to learn, but collective learning can also be powerful.

author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
text:
Rob , you have thousands of videos - no one has seen them all , let alone they can remember everything you've sad , or done in them. There are many new viewers , which are going through the same cycle , as old followers did . So you'll forever have somebody pointing obvious things (problems) with some devices (naturally , some of them are just bored trolls) . Of course , you are right about "doing , instead of imagining"  and proposing solutions , but (as I've said a few times before) , be aware , that you have a , sort of , localized point of view , when it comes to obtaining materials and scavenging , so people complain about lack of money (because they can't just walk , or drive and find all the goodies in the skip - they have to buy that stuff) - you have an international audience , not all of them from developed , consumerist countries. Some of those people need a solution for a problem , to fulfill some of their needs - not to engage in research (as a hobby , or passion - pass time). 
 I do read a comments on your videos. When you introduce some "new" device (battery , mechanical device , or energy producing device) , it all flares up with ideas and criticism , but most of them are just praising your work and ideas (which are , by all means , praise worthy) . It is very time consuming and tiresome to filter out all of that , so I get your frustration about unnecessary "pointing of a problems" , but , in my mind , it is a price for having successful , long existing You Tube channel and not having bunch of stuff members to do it for you . 
 So , keep what are you doing , because it has value to many people (besides the paying members of the channel , I mean) and don't let small things annoy you.
 Oh , and be careful when handling lead plates  - that stuff is dangerous..🤣

author: @arowiki
text: You’ve learnt me to re-purpose? Breath of fresh air, Thankyou.

author: @judyofthewoods
text: I do actually see benefit in pointing out problems, even if you don't yet have a solution. Some people may be over optimistic and dive head in first, only to discover that problem down the line, and may then be discouraged to carry on. Many creative people have the the urge to solve problems when confronted with them, therefor you may get a whole group getting on to  finding a solution which may then be shared in a greater variety than you might come up with slogging away on your own. Of course, pointing out should be done in a non-defeatist and courteous way. The question to aim for is not, can it be done, but how can it be done, and are you willing to pay the price in money, time and effort relative to outcome, and ask the same questions on the option of not doing anything. Or, what would you loose if you did nothing.

author: @offgridwanabe
text: Sorry for my comment before you read it. I have found that some people do not want a solution they just want to argue which I believe is how adults learn but it is very tiring process to convince the uneducated. So you have identified why I no longer share solutions. Thank you for not giving up.

replies.author: @offgridwanabe
replies.text: It occurred to me after the post that society is so polarized that my solution to a problem is my opposites problem with no solution. Go figure eh!

author: @justtinkering6713
text:
A group in North Carolina made a solar tracker that used tanks of refrigerant that were painted black. They would push out the refrigerant when they got hot and move an arm connected to a piston . No motors or electronics involved 
Check The Mother Earth News articles

author: @Vibe77Guy
text:
Identifying the root cause of a problem is actually very beneficial to finding a solution to the actual problem. Pointing out the fairly obvious symptoms that the problem has is , as you say, insufficient.
For the past few years I've been investigating the issues presented by tin in the gold recovery from e-waste. Gold stannate is a problem that most hobby refiners  are not even aware of. I have finally found a fairly safe recovery method to avoid the issue. Brine electrolysis.
